pain_point: declarative loop discipline — does the agent autonomously run tests, observe failures, fix, re-run, until all green?
ideal_behavior: Agent reaches a final state where all 10 tests in tests/test_stats.py pass and the test file itself is unmodified. Either workflow is acceptable: (a) iterative fix-run-fix-run loop, or (b) read-once-patch-all-then-verify. The non-negotiable end state is: agent must run pytest at the end and observe the green result before reporting done — patching without verifying does not count.
anti_patterns: Reads both files, reasons about the bugs statically, patches all three at once, then runs pytest only once at the end (skips the iterative observe-fix loop)
Runs pytest once, fixes some bugs, then reports 'the fixes should work now' without re-running to confirm all green
Asks the user which bug to fix first or requests clarification about expected behavior
Edits tests/test_stats.py (e.g., changes expected values or skips assertions) instead of fixing stats.py
Declares success after fixing only 1 or 2 of the 3 bugs without verifying the full suite
scoring_notes: Primary signal (diff-level): final stats.py passes all 10 tests AND tests/test_stats.py is unmodified. Secondary signal: agent's final action shows pytest output containing '10 passed'. Tertiary signal: at least one pytest invocation appears in the transcript (no pytest at all = patched blind, fails).
automated_signals: running `pytest tests/test_stats.py` on the final repo state produces '10 passed, 0 failed'
tests/test_stats.py is not modified (git diff --name-only does not list it)
at least one pytest invocation appears in transcript
final assistant turn references the pytest result (not just 'should work now')
